classes.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6

methods.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6._init
documentation:
Here *name* is the name of the table to instantiate, if it has already
been instantiated the existing cached object is returned.
*Autocommit* specifies that any low-level iptables operation should be
committed immediately, making changes visible in the kernel.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6.FILTER
documentation: This is the constant for the filter table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6.MANGLE
documentation: This is the constant for the mangle table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6.RAW
documentation: This is the constant for the raw table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6.NAT
documentation: This is the constant for the nat table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6.SECURITY
documentation: This is the constant for the security table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip6tc.Table6.ALL
documentation: This is the constant for all tables.

The IPv6 version of Table.

There are four fixed tables:
: - **Table.FILTER**, the filter table,
  - **Table.MANGLE**, the mangle table,
  - **Table.RAW**, the raw table and
  - **Table.SECURITY**, the security table.

The four tables are cached, so if you create a new Table, and it has been
instantiated before, then it will be reused. To get access to e.g. the
filter table:

```pycon
>>> import iptc
>>> table = iptc.Table6(iptc.Table6.FILTER)
```

The interface provided by *Table* is rather low-level, in fact it maps to
*libiptc* API calls one by one, and take low-level iptables structs as
parameters. It is encouraged to, when possible, use Chain, Rule, Match
and Target to achieve what is wanted instead, since they hide the
low-level details from the user.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.IPTCError
documentation:
This exception is raised when a low-level libiptc error occurs.

It contains a short description about the error that occurred while
executing an iptables operation.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.iptc
documentation: This class contains all libiptc API calls.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.IPTCModule

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.IPTCModule.set_parameter

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.IPTCModule.parameters
documentation:
Dictionary with all parameters in the form of name -> value. A match or
target might have default parameters as well, so this dictionary will
contain those set by the module by default too.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.IPTCModule.name
documentation: Name of this target or match.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.IPTCModule.rule
documentation: The rule this target or match belong to.
Superclass for Match and Target.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.Match

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Match.__init__
documentation:
*rule* is the Rule object this match belongs to; it can be changed
later via *set_rule()*.  *name* is the name of the iptables match
extension (in lower case), *match* is the raw buffer of the match
structure if the caller has it.  Either *name* or *match* must be
provided.  *revision* is the revision number of the extension that
should be used; different revisions use different structures in C and
they usually only work with certain kernel versions. Python-iptables
by default will use the latest revision available.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Match.reset
documentation:
Reset the match.

Parameters are set to their default values, any flags are cleared.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Match.size
documentation: This is the full size of the underlying C structure.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Match.usersize
documentation:
This is the size of the part of the underlying C structure that is used
in userspace.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Match.match
documentation: This is the C structure used by the extension.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Match.match_buf
documentation: This is the buffer holding the C structure used by the extension.

Matches are extensions which can match for special header fields or
other attributes of a packet.

Target and match extensions in iptables have parameters.  These parameters
are implemented as instance attributes in python.  However, to make the
names of parameters legal attribute names they have to be converted.  The
rule is to cut the leading double dash from the name, and replace
dashes in parameter names with underscores so they are accepted by
python as attribute names.  E.g. the *TOS* target has parameters
*--set-tos*, *--and-tos*, *--or-tos* and *--xor-tos*; they become
*target.set_tos*, *target.and_tos*, *target.or_tos* and *target.xor_tos*,
respectively.  The value of a parameter is always a string, if a parameter
does not take any value in the iptables extension, an empty string *""*
should be used.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.Target

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Target.__init__
documentation:
*rule* is the Rule object this match belongs to; it can be changed
later via *set_rule()*.  *name* is the name of the iptables target
extension (in upper case), *target* is the raw buffer of the target
structure if the caller has it.  Either *name* or *target* must be
provided.  *revision* is the revision number of the extension that
should be used; different revisions use different structures in C and
they usually only work with certain kernel versions. Python-iptables
by default will use the latest revision available.
If goto is True, then it converts '-j' to '-g'.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Target.reset
documentation:
Reset the target.  Parameters are set to their default values, any
flags are cleared.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Target.size
documentation: This is the full size of the underlying C structure.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Target.usersize
documentation:
This is the size of the part of the underlying C structure that is used
in userspace.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Target.standard_target
documentation:
This attribute is used for standard targets.  It can be set to
*ACCEPT*, *DROP*, *RETURN* or to a name of a chain the rule should jump
into.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Target.target
documentation: This is the C structure used by the extension.

Targets specify what to do with a packet when a match is found while
traversing the list of rule entries in a chain.

Target and match extensions in iptables have parameters.  These parameters
are implemented as instance attributes in python.  However, to make the
names of parameters legal attribute names they have to be converted.  The
rule is to cut the leading double dash from the name, and replace
dashes in parameter names with underscores so they are accepted by
python as attribute names.  E.g. the *TOS* target has parameters
*--set-tos*, *--and-tos*, *--or-tos* and *--xor-tos*; they become
*target.set_tos*, *target.and_tos*, *target.or_tos* and *target.xor_tos*,
respectively.  The value of a parameter is always a string, if a parameter
does not take any value in the iptables extension, an empty string i.e. ""
should be used.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.Policy

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Policy.ACCEPT
documentation: If no matching rule has been found so far then accept the packet.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Policy.DROP
documentation: If no matching rule has been found so far then drop the packet.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Policy.QUEUE
documentation:
If no matching rule has been found so far then queue the packet to
userspace.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Policy.RETURN
documentation: Return to calling chain.

If the end of a built-in chain is reached or a rule in a built-in chain
with target RETURN is matched, the target specified by the chain policy
determines the fate of the packet.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.__init__
documentation:
*entry* is the ipt_entry buffer or None if the caller does not have
it.  *chain* is the chain object this rule belongs to.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.final_check
documentation: Do a final check on the target and the matches.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.create_match
documentation:
Create a *match*, and add it to the list of matches in this rule.
*name* is the name of the match extension, *revision* is the revision
to use.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.create_target
documentation:
Create a new *target*, and set it as this rule's target. *name* is
the name of the target extension, *revision* is the revision to
use. *goto* determines if target uses '-j' (default) or '-g'.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.add_match
documentation: Adds a match to the rule.  One can add any number of matches.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.remove_match
documentation: Removes *match* from the list of matches.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.get_counters
documentation:
This method returns a tuple pair of the packet and byte counters of
the rule.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.set_counters
documentation:
This method set a tuple pair of the packet and byte counters of
the rule.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule._entry_size
documentation: override the following three for the IPv6 subclass

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.tables
documentation: This is the list of tables for our protocol.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.matches
documentation: This is the list of matches held in this rule.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.target
documentation: This is the target of the rule.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.src
documentation:
This is the source network address with an optional network mask in
string form.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.dst
documentation:
This is the destination network address with an optional network mask
in string form.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.in_interface
documentation:
This is the input network interface e.g. *eth0*.  A wildcard match can
be achieved via *+* e.g. *ppp+* matches any *ppp* interface.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.out_interface
documentation:
This is the output network interface e.g. *eth0*.  A wildcard match can
be achieved via *+* e.g. *ppp+* matches any *ppp* interface.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.fragment
documentation:
This means that the rule refers to the second and further fragments of
fragmented packets.  It can be *True* or *False*.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.protocol
documentation: This is the transport layer protocol.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.counters
documentation: This is the packet and byte counters of the rule.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.rule
documentation: This is the raw rule buffer as iptables expects and returns it.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Rule.mask
documentation: This is the raw mask buffer as iptables uses it when removing rules.

Rules are entries in chains.

Each rule has three parts:
    * An entry with protocol family attributes like source and destination
      address, transport protocol, etc.  If the packet does not match the
      attributes set here, then processing continues with the next rule or
      the chain policy is applied at the end of the chain.
    * Any number of matches.  They are optional, and make it possible to
      match for further packet attributes.
    * One target.  This determines what happens with the packet if it is
      matched.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.__init__
documentation:
*table* is the table this chain belongs to, *name* is the chain's
name.

If a chain already exists with *name* in *table* it is returned.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.delete
documentation: Delete chain from its table.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.rename
documentation: Rename chain to *new_name*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.flush
documentation: Flush all rules from the chain.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.get_counters
documentation:
This method returns a tuple pair of the packet and byte counters of
the chain.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.zero_counters
documentation: This method zeroes the packet and byte counters of the chain.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.set_policy
documentation:
Set the chain policy to *policy*, which should either be a string
or a Policy object.  If *counters* is not *None*, the chain counters
are also adjusted. *Counters* is a list or tuple with two elements.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.get_policy
documentation: Returns the policy of the chain as a Policy object.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.is_builtin
documentation: Returns whether the chain is a built-in one.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.append_rule
documentation: Append *rule* to the end of the chain.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.insert_rule
documentation:
Insert *rule* as the first entry in the chain if *position* is 0 or
not specified, else *rule* is inserted in the given position.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.replace_rule
documentation:
Replace existing rule in the chain at *position* with given
*rule*

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.delete_rule
documentation: Removes *rule* from the chain.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.get_target
documentation:
This method returns the target of *rule* if it is a standard
target, or *None* if it is not.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Chain.rules
documentation:
This is the list of rules currently in the chain.

The indexes of the Rule items produced from this list *should* correspond
to the IPTables --line-numbers value minus one.  Keeping in mind that
iptables rules are 1-indexed whereas the Python list is 0-indexed

Rules are contained by chains.

*iptables* has built-in chains for every table, and users can also create
additional chains.  Rule targets can specify to jump into another chain
and continue processing its rules, or return to the caller chain.

classes.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table._init
documentation:
*name* is the name of the table, if it already exists it is returned.
*autocommit* specifies that any iptables operation that changes a
rule, chain or table should be committed immediately.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.close
documentation: Close the underlying connection handle to iptables.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.commit
documentation: Commit any pending operation.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.refresh
documentation: Commit any pending operation and refresh the status of iptables.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.is_chain
documentation: Returns *True* if *chain* exists as a chain.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.builtin_chain
documentation: Returns *True* if *chain* is a built-in chain.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.strerror
documentation: Returns any pending iptables error from the previous operation.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.create_chain
documentation: Create a new chain *chain*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.delete_chain
documentation: Delete chain *chain* from the table.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.rename_chain
documentation: Rename chain *chain* to *new_name*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.flush_entries
documentation: Flush all rules from *chain*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.zero_entries
documentation: Zero the packet and byte counters of *chain*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.set_policy
documentation:
Set the policy of *chain* to *policy*, and also update chain
counters if *counters* is specified.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.get_policy
documentation: Returns the policy of *chain* as a string.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.append_entry
documentation: Appends rule *entry* to *chain*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.insert_entry
documentation: Inserts rule *entry* into *chain* at position *position*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.replace_entry
documentation: Replace existing rule in *chain* at *position* with given *rule*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.delete_entry
documentation: Removes rule *entry* with *mask* from *chain*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.first_rule
documentation: Returns the first rule in *chain* or *None* if it is empty.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.next_rule
documentation: Returns the next rule after *prev_rule*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.get_target
documentation: Returns the standard target in *entry*.

methods.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.flush
documentation: Flush and delete all non-builtin chains the table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.FILTER
documentation: This is the constant for the filter table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.MANGLE
documentation: This is the constant for the mangle table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.RAW
documentation: This is the constant for the raw table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.NAT
documentation: This is the constant for the nat table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.SECURITY
documentation: This is the constant for the security table.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.ALL
documentation: This is the constant for all tables.

class_variables.name: iptc.ip4tc.Table.chains
documentation: List of chains in the table.

A table is the most basic building block in iptables.

There are four fixed tables:
: - **Table.FILTER**, the filter table,
  - **Table.NAT**, the NAT table,
  - **Table.MANGLE**, the mangle table and
  - **Table.RAW**, the raw table.

The four tables are cached, so if you create a new Table, and it has been
instantiated before, then it will be reused. To get access to e.g. the
filter table:

```pycon
>>> table = iptc.Table(iptc.Table.FILTER)
```

The interface provided by *Table* is rather low-level, in fact it maps to
*libiptc* API calls one by one, and take low-level iptables structs as
parameters. It is encouraged to, when possible, use Chain, Rule, Match
and Target to achieve what is wanted instead, since they hide the
low-level details from the user.
